not_buildable_units.A2-systems: Three of five deliverables are reference-grade: (1) profiling/benchmarking — torch.profiler with schedule/warmup/sync, nsys/ncu command lines, NVTX, roofline classification of measured kernels, MFU accounting, memory snapshots in 04/10, and an all-reduce bandwidth benchmark with the busbw formula; (3) correctness testing — every kernel in the book ships with a reference test, tolerance guidance, T

deficits.missing: Line 37 claims 'Common Crawl's crawlers already ran trafilatura or a similar extraction tool' to produce WET files. This is factually wrong: Common Crawl WET files are produced by its own simple text extractor (historically Apache Tika-based), not trafilatura. This misleads a reader into thinking WET already has trafilatura-quality extraction, which is a primary reason FineWeb/RefinedWeb re-extract from WARC.
deficits.fix: Correct the claim: state that CC WET uses a basic built-in extractor whose quality is lower than modern extractors, which is exactly why high-quality pipelines (RefinedWeb, FineWeb) re-extract from WARC with trafilatura/resiliparse.

deficits.missing: The parse_wet_records() code contaminates every extracted document: (a) the 'Content-Length: N' header line is appended to the body because in real WET records Content-Length follows Content-Type, and the parser sets in_body=True upon seeing Content-Type; (b) the NEXT record's 'WARC/1.0' and 'WARC-Type: conversion' header lines are appended to the PREVIOUS document's text because in_body is never reset until WARC-Target-URI is seen. Every yielded document carries a 'Content-Length:' prefix line and a 'WARC/1.0\nWARC-Type: conversion' suffix.
deficits.fix: Rewrite the parser to (1) split records on the 'WARC/1.0' record boundary, (2) parse headers until the first blank line, (3) read exactly Content-Length bytes for the body (or accumulate body lines until the next 'WARC/1.0'), and reset all per-record state when a new record starts. Add a unit test on a 2-record sample WET string with exact expected (url, text) output.

deficits.missing: The book jumps directly to WET files (already-extracted plain text) and never covers extracting text from raw WARC HTML. Deliverable #1 and CS336 Assignment 4 explicitly require going from raw WARC (full HTTP responses / HTML) to text. There is no HTML-to-text extraction code, no encoding/charset detection, no boilerplate stripping, and no use of warcio/fastwarc/resiliparse/trafilatura. The single trafilatura mention is a passing (and incorrect) aside about what Common Crawl already did.
deficits.fix: Add a section 'Extracting text from WARC' with real code: iterate WARC response records with fastwarc/warcio, detect charset, run an HTML-to-text extractor (resiliparse or trafilatura) to strip boilerplate, and compare WARC-derived vs WET-derived text quality. This is the actual first stage of the CS336 assignment.

deficits.missing: Chapter claims 'Common Crawl's crawlers already ran trafilatura or a similar extraction tool' to produce WET files. This is wrong: WET text comes from Common Crawl's own basic extractor (ia-web-commons/jsoup-based), and a central finding of RefinedWeb and FineWeb is that re-extracting from WARC HTML with trafilatura substantially outperforms WET text quality. The claim inverts the actual state of practice.
deficits.fix: Correct the claim, and add a subsection on WARC-based extraction: reading WARC records with warcio, extracting main content with trafilatura/resiliparse, and note FineWeb's ablation that trafilatura-on-WARC beat WET-based extraction. Include a short warcio+trafilatura code example, since this is the extraction path used by RefinedWeb, FineWeb, and Dolma.

deficits.missing: No coverage at all of HTML-to-text extraction from raw WARC files (boilerplate removal, main-content extraction). The book's only extraction path is parsing pre-extracted WET plain text; a reader cannot reproduce the modern pipeline (RefinedWeb/FineWeb/Dolma all extract from WARC, not WET).
deficits.fix: Add a 'From WARC to text' section with working code (warcio iterator over response records, trafilatura.extract on the HTML payload, charset handling) and a comparison table WET-text vs trafilatura-text on the same page.

deficits.missing: parse_wet_records is a hand-rolled line-scanner that delimits records by the next 'WARC-Target-URI:' header and ignores Content-Length; it is fragile (body lines that resemble headers, the leading warcinfo record, multi-blank-line bodies) and no robust alternative is shown.
deficits.fix: Note the fragility and show the production-standard path (warcio/fastwarc ArchiveIterator honoring Content-Length) alongside the didactic parser, or at least add a caveat that real pipelines must not rely on header-string heuristics.

deficits.missing: C4 description says 'deduplicate at the 3-gram level'. C4's dedup removed duplicate three-SENTENCE spans (any span of 3 sentences occurring more than once), not 3-grams.
deficits.fix: Change to 'deduplicated at the three-sentence-span level (any three-sentence span occurring more than once in the corpus was removed)'.

deficits.missing: RefinedWeb is named in the unit's learning objectives but has no case study in the 'Open Dataset Case Studies' section (Pile, C4, RedPajama, Dolma, FineWeb are covered); RefinedWeb appears only in passing in chapter 3.2's heuristics table and references, and its MacroData Refinement pipeline (URL filtering, trafilatura extraction, MassiveText-style filters, exact+fuzzy dedup) is never described.
deficits.fix: Add a RefinedWeb case study paragraph to the Open Dataset Case Studies section describing the MDR pipeline stages and the 'web data alone can beat curated mixes' finding it established before FineWeb.

deficits.missing: Writer/reader inconsistency: the practitioner tip says 'Write shards with context_len + 1 tokens per row', and ShardedTokenDataset reads rows of (context_len + 1), but TokenShard._flush_shard aligns shards to plain ctx_len boundaries — the tip is never implemented. It works only accidentally because tokens within a shard are contiguous. Also ShardedTokenDataset (IterableDataset) has no worker sharding: with DataLoader(num_workers>1) every worker yields the full dataset, duplicating data.
deficits.fix: Make TokenShard pack rows of (ctx_len + 1) as the tip prescribes (or delete the alignment logic and explain the contiguous-stream reading), and add torch.utils.data.get_worker_info() shard-splitting to ShardedTokenDataset with a note about the multi-worker duplication pitfall.

deficits.missing: The main pipeline has no verification artifacts: no sample WET fixture with expected document counts/retention, no expected token counts after tokenize+pack, no sanity check that a decoded shard row round-trips to text. (Ch 3.2's MinHash demo with known near-dup structure is good; ch 3.1 has nothing comparable.) Also the LSH worked example says P=0.954 for s=0.8; the correct value is 1-(0.832)^16 = 0.947.
deficits.fix: Add a tiny inline test: a 3-record WET string constant, expected parsed output, expected filter pass/fail per record, and expected shard token count; fix 0.954 -> 0.947.

deficits.missing: The WET parser appends subsequent header lines (e.g. 'Content-Length: 2048') to the document body: after 'Content-Type: text/plain' sets in_body=True, the following header lines match no branch except the final 'elif in_body' and are appended to body_lines; the blank-separator skip then also fails because body_lines is no longer empty.
deficits.fix: Parse headers until the first blank line, then collect body lines until the next 'WARC/1.0' record delimiter; or explicitly skip lines matching '^[A-Za-z-]+:' while in the header block.

deficits.missing: The MFU worked example is arithmetically wrong: 1.68e7 tokens in 2.4 s = 7.0e6 tok/s gives 6N*tok/s = 281 PFLOP/s vs 63.4 PFLOP/s peak, i.e. MFU = 4.44 (444%, physically impossible), yet the text concludes '0.44 = 44%'. The premise throughput is ~10x too high. The same example also contains a leftover editing artifact ('= 6.3x10^16 x ... = 6.34x10^16? Recompute:') in the peak-FLOPs line.
deficits.fix: Change the measured step to e.g. 1.68e6 tokens in 2.4 s (or 1.68e7 tokens in 24 s) so throughput is 0.7e6 tok/s and MFU genuinely computes to 44%; delete the garbled 'Recompute' sentence and state peak cleanly as 64 x 990 TFLOP/s = 63.4 PFLOP/s.

deficits.missing: The 'Worked example: MFU of a concrete training step' is arithmetically wrong and describes a physically impossible scenario. 7B model, 64 H100s, 4096 seqs x 4096 tokens in 2.4 s gives 6.99e6 tok/s -> 6N*tok/s = 281 PFLOP/s vs 63.4 PFLOP/s peak, i.e. MFU = 4.43 (443%), not the claimed 0.44 = 44%. The division 281/63.4 is off by 10x, and the example also contains a leftover self-correction artifact ('Peak: ... = 6.34x10^16? Recompute: ...') in the published prose.
deficits.fix: Make the scenario physically consistent: keep 64 H100s and 44% target MFU but change the step to 4096 seqs x 4096 tokens in 24 s (0.70e6 tok/s -> 281/634... recompute: 6*6.7e9*0.699e6 = 2.81e16 FLOP/s vs 6.34e16 peak = 44%), or keep 2.4 s and use 640 GPUs. Then delete the 'Recompute:' artifact and re-verify the printed numbers with the chapter's own mfu() function.

deficits.missing: The MFU worked example contains a leftover self-correction artifact: 'Peak: 64 x 990x10^12 = 6.3x10^16 x ... = 6.34x10^16? Recompute: ...'. The final number (63.4 PFLOP/s) is correct, but the mangled first attempt is left in the published prose.
deficits.fix: Delete the false start and keep only the clean computation: 64 x 990 TFLOP/s = 63,360 TFLOP/s = 63.4 PFLOP/s.

deficits.missing: Editing artifact left in the MFU worked example (line ~446): 'Peak: 64 x 990x10^12 = 6.3x10^16 x ... = 6.34x10^16? Recompute: ...' — a self-correction draft note published verbatim.
deficits.fix: Delete the garbled sentence and keep only the correct computation: 64 x 990 TFLOP/s = 63.4 PFLOP/s.

deficits.missing: The 'Worked example: MinHash parameter selection' evaluates (1/16)^{1/8} as approx 0.794; the correct value is 0.707. It also states P(candidate | s=0.8) approx 1-0.046 = 0.954; the correct value is 1-0.053 = 0.947. The formula t* approx (1/b)^{1/r} is stated correctly, but the evaluated numbers are wrong, so a reader following the example believes b=16,r=8 targets threshold ~0.8 when it actually targets ~0.71 (more false-positive candidate pairs than the text implies).
deficits.fix: Correct the arithmetic: t* = (1/16)^{1/8} = 16^{-0.125} approx 0.707, and P(candidate|s=0.8) approx 0.947. Either keep b=16,r=8 and note the effective threshold is ~0.71 (conservative, catches more candidates than the 0.8 target, filtered later by signature verification), or show the parameter choice that actually yields t* approx 0.8 (e.g. b=9,r=14 with k=126, or b=8,r=16 giving t* approx 0.878 with k=128 and discuss the trade-off).

deficits.missing: Deliverable #4 ('measure the downstream effect of filtering choices on model quality') is only discussed qualitatively (Lee et al. 1.5-2x sample efficiency, FineWeb/DCLM ablation citations, yield tables). There is no experimental protocol or runnable harness a reader could execute to actually MEASURE the effect: no train-a-small-LM-on-filtered-vs-unfiltered loop, no held-out eval/benchmark comparison, no delta-reporting methodology, no compute budget for the ablation.
deficits.fix: Add a worked ablation harness: take two data subsets (e.g. raw vs filtered+deduped), train a small (~100M-160M) model on each for a fixed token budget, evaluate held-out perplexity plus a small downstream benchmark (e.g. a subset of HellaSwag/PIQA), and report the metric delta. Include the exact eval loop and expected magnitude of improvement so the reader can reproduce and validate.

deficits.missing: Bloom filters as a dedup mechanism (the unit objective explicitly lists them, and Dolma's dedup is Bloom-filter based) are only name-dropped in tool listings ('Rust Bloom-filter dedup'). The mechanism -- bit array of m bits, k hash functions, false-positive rate p approx (1-e^{-kn/m})^k, optimal k = (m/n)ln2, no false negatives, and why that asymmetry is acceptable for dedup (a false positive drops a unique doc; tune p accordingly) -- is never explained. The pybloom_live usage in 13-interp-safety-gov/03 is a black-box library call in a different context.
deficits.fix: Add a short 'Bloom-filter dedup' subsection to 3.2 between exact dedup and MinHash: derive the FP-rate formula, give the m/k sizing math for a concrete case (e.g. 1B documents at p=1e-6 needs ~28.8 bits/element approx 3.6 GB vs 32 GB of SHA-256 hashes), a ~30-line from-scratch implementation (bitarray + k mmh3/md5-derived hashes), and note this is exactly what allenai/dolma uses for both exact-doc and paragraph dedup.

deficits.missing: The quality-score formula at line 155 writes score(d) = exp(-(1/N) sum log P)^{-1}, which is the INVERSE of perplexity, while the surrounding text says 'a lower perplexity means the document resembles Wikipedia-quality text.' The labeled quantity and the prose disagree, and the extra ^{-1} is unexplained.
deficits.fix: Either define perplexity cleanly as PPL = exp(-(1/N) sum log P) and say keep low-PPL docs, or if defining an inverted score make the direction (higher score = better) explicit and consistent.

deficits.missing: Line 26 comment 'suppress_stdou=True silences fastText's noisy banner' has a typo and references a parameter that is never passed to fasttext.load_model (which takes no such argument). Cosmetic but misleading in code a reader will copy.
deficits.fix: Remove the erroneous comment or show the correct way to suppress fastText output (e.g. fasttext.FastText.eprint = lambda *a, **k: None).

deficits.missing: Inconsistent and inflated WET size figures: ch 3.1 says each monthly dump is '20-30 TB of WET files' and the interview answer says '~25 TB compressed'; ch 3.2 says 'one full CommonCrawl snapshot (roughly 80 TB of compressed WET files)'. Recent crawls are roughly 90-100 TB compressed WARC but only ~8-10 TB compressed WET.
deficits.fix: Reconcile to one consistent, accurate set of numbers (e.g., ~90 TB WARC / ~9 TB WET per recent monthly crawl) across both chapters.

deficits.missing: Suffix-array section claims a 100-billion-token corpus needs '400 GB of RAM (4 bytes per token index)'. A 32-bit index addresses only ~4.3 billion positions; 100B token positions require at least 5-byte (in practice 8-byte) indices, i.e. ~500-800 GB.
deficits.fix: Correct the index-width arithmetic (int64 indices, ~800 GB for 100B tokens) — this actually strengthens the argument for sharded suffix arrays that follows.

deficits.missing: The MinHash-LSH demo (doc1/doc2/doc5 near-dups) prints results but gives no expected output values or assertions, unlike the DoReMi toy (which prints expected numbers) and the LangID snippet (which has an assert). A reader cannot confirm their implementation is correct against the book.
deficits.fix: Add the expected demo output (which pairs are found and approximate estimated Jaccard values, e.g. doc1<->doc2 approx 0.7-0.9) plus one assertion comparing jaccard_from_sigs against exact Jaccard computed on the shingle sets, with the expected estimation-error bound approx 1/sqrt(k).

deficits.missing: minhash_signature initializes entries to float('inf') and calls int(v) at the end; an empty shingle set (empty/whitespace-only document, or document shorter than shingle length k) raises OverflowError at int(float('inf')). The pipeline never guards against short documents before shingling.
deficits.fix: Guard the edge case: if not shingles, return a sentinel signature or skip the document (documents shorter than the shingle length should be dropped or exact-deduped only); mention this in the code comments.

deficits.missing: The KenLM quality-score formula score(d) = exp(-1/N sum log P)^{-1} is confusing: exp(-mean log P) is already perplexity, so the extra ^{-1} makes score the inverse perplexity, but the surrounding prose talks about 'lower perplexity' as if score were perplexity itself.
deficits.fix: Either drop the ^{-1} and say 'keep documents with perplexity below a threshold', or keep the inverse and consistently call it a score where higher is better. One sentence tying the formula to the CCNet percentile-threshold practice would remove the ambiguity.

deficits.missing: The end-to-end quality classifier is described (fastText Wikipedia-vs-CommonCrawl, KenLM percentile) but the only trainable classifier code in the book is the FineWeb-Edu Ridge head in 3.15, which depends on an unspecified embed_fn and lives in the synthetic-data chapter where a reader auditing 'quality filtering' may not look.
deficits.fix: Add to 3.2 a compact end-to-end trainable example: build labels (Wikipedia paragraphs = positive, raw CC = negative), train fasttext.train_supervised (or sklearn LogisticRegression on hashed n-gram features), pick the retention percentile on a held-out set, and cross-reference the FineWeb-Edu Ridge-head recipe in 3.15 (specifying a concrete embed_fn, e.g. a named sentence-transformers model).

deficits.missing: No Triton FlashAttention BACKWARD kernel anywhere in the book. The backward algorithm is fully derived and implemented in NumPy (FA1 chapter, with grad check), but the Triton chapter's Kernel 4 is forward-only and explicitly defers backward to 'the FlashAttention chapters', which only give NumPy. The Triton mechanics the backward specifically needs are never taught: tl.atomic_add (or the two-kernel dKdV/dQ split), a preprocessing kernel computing D_i = rowsum(dO*O), and how the forward must store the per-row logsumexp L (the shown Triton forward never writes L).
deficits.fix: Add a 'Kernel 5: FlashAttention backward' section: (1) modify the forward kernel to compute and tl.store L = m_i + log2/ln-consistent logsumexp; (2) a small preprocess kernel computing D = rowsum(dO*O); (3) a backward kernel parallel over KV blocks producing dK/dV locally and accumulating dQ via tl.atomic_add (introduce the primitive), mirroring the FA2 paper's partitioning the FA2-3 chapter already describes in prose; (4) correctness test against torch.autograd on an fp32 reference with stated tolerances.

deficits.missing: The Triton FlashAttention kernel is non-causal only. Causal masking is described in one prose note ('break the loop at the diagonal block and apply tl.where(offs_m[:,None] >= offs_n[None,:], s, -inf)') but never shown in working kernel code, and the fully-masked-row edge case (l_i == 0 guard, handled in the NumPy version) is not addressed for the Triton version.
deficits.fix: Add the causal variant to the kernel code: pass a CAUSAL: tl.constexpr flag, compute the loop bound hi = (start_m+1)*BLOCK_M for causal, apply the triangular tl.where on the diagonal block, and extend the correctness test to causal=True. Note the l_i==0 guard needed if BLOCK_M rows can be fully masked.

deficits.missing: No demonstration of wiring custom forward/backward kernels into PyTorch autograd (torch.autograd.Function with ctx.save_for_backward). Without it a reader cannot actually train with their Triton attention kernel, which is the point of the assignment.
deficits.fix: Add a short section defining class FlashAttentionFn(torch.autograd.Function) with forward saving (q,k,v,o,L) via ctx.save_for_backward and backward invoking the Triton backward kernels, plus a torch.autograd.gradcheck-style or analytic-vs-reference gradient test.

deficits.missing: No Triton backward kernel anywhere in the book. The backward algorithm is fully derived and gradient-checked in NumPy (ch 4.2) and the kv-parallel loop-ordering strategy is stated in prose (ch 4.3, line 122), but the deliverable 'Triton FA-2 forward and backward kernel' requires the reader to synthesize the entire Triton backward (D-preprocessing, kv-parallel dK/dV kernel, q-parallel or atomic dQ) from scratch.
deficits.fix: Add a 'Kernel 5: FlashAttention backward' section (or a fully-guided exercise with skeleton code) implementing the two-kernel backward: a preprocessing step computing D = rowsum(dO*O), a kv-parallel kernel producing dK/dV via load-time-transposed Q/dO tiles, and a q-parallel dQ kernel, each verified against torch.autograd.

deficits.missing: Kernel 4 (the Triton FlashAttention forward) does not compute or store the per-row logsumexp L = m_i + log(l_i), which chapter 4.2 establishes as the one statistic the backward pass requires. As written, no backward can be attached to this forward without modifying it.
deficits.fix: Add an L_ptr output to flash_attn_kernel and store m_i + tl.log(l_i) (or the base-2 equivalent) after the KV loop, with a comment tying it to the backward-pass recomputation in ch 4.2.

deficits.missing: The Triton FA kernel hardcodes BLOCK_M=BLOCK_N=64 with no autotuning and no SRAM/register budget analysis for the attention kernel (the matmul kernel gets a worked SRAM example; attention does not), and requires HEAD_DIM to be a tl.constexpr power-of-two without saying what to do for e.g. d=80.
deficits.fix: Add an @triton.autotune config list for the attention kernel and a one-paragraph SRAM budget (Q tile + K/V tiles + fp32 acc) analogous to the matmul worked example; note padding of head_dim to the next power of two.

deficits.missing: The exp2 trick (folding log2(e) into qk_scale) puts the running max m_i in base-2 log space. The interaction between this and the saved logsumexp L (which the ch-4.2 backward defines in natural-log space) is never discussed; a reader combining the two chapters can silently produce an inconsistent-base L and wrong gradients.
deficits.fix: Add a note stating that with the exp2 trick, L must be stored as (m_i + tl.log2(l_i))/log2(e) to recover the natural-log logsumexp, or that the backward must use exp2 with the same folded scale consistently.

deficits.missing: Causal masking is described precisely in prose (loop break at the diagonal block plus tl.where(offs_m[:,None] >= offs_n[None,:], s, -inf)) but is not implemented in the Triton kernel, and the FA-2 deliverable implies causal support. The two-phase loop structure (full blocks then masked diagonal block) that real kernels use is not shown in code.
deficits.fix: Add a causal variant of Kernel 4 (or an exercise with the exact loop bound `range(0, (start_m+1)*BLOCK_M, BLOCK_N)` and the diagonal-block mask) verified against a causal fp32 reference.

deficits.missing: tl.atomic_add and tl.trans are never introduced anywhere in the book, yet the stated backward strategies (ch 4.2: 'dQ is accumulated with atomics') reference atomics. A reader following that prose route has no Triton primitive to reach for; the no-atomics alternative (load-time transposes via stride swapping, separate dQ kernel) is derivable but never made explicit.
deficits.fix: Introduce tl.atomic_add (and note tl.trans) in the Triton-language survey, and state explicitly that transposed operands can be obtained by swapping the roles of stride offsets in the pointer computation, as Kernel 4 already does implicitly for K.

deficits.missing: Optimizer state sharding (ZeRO-1) has a precise 4-step protocol description (reduce-scatter grads, update owned shard, all-gather updated params) and memory accounting, plus library-level FSDP usage — but no from-scratch implementation, unlike DDP which gets the excellent TinyDDP. Left unspecified in code: how to partition parameters across ranks (flat-buffer slice vs per-parameter assignment), handling world-size-indivisible parameter counts, and constructing the wrapped torch.optim instance over only owned shards.
deficits.fix: Add a ~80-line TinyShardedOptimizer companion to TinyDDP: greedy per-parameter assignment to ranks (or flat slicing), each rank runs AdamW over its owned params only, then dist.broadcast (from owner) or all-gather of updated params; include a test asserting bit-identical parameter trajectories versus unsharded AdamW after N steps, and a per-rank memory measurement showing the ~12 bytes/param saving.

deficits.missing: TinyDDP has a usage loop but no correctness verification: nothing asserts that replicas stay in lockstep or that the result matches single-process training on the concatenated batch.
deficits.fix: Add a test: run the same data through a single-process model with the full batch and assert torch.allclose of parameters against the TinyDDP run after several steps; also assert all-rank parameter equality via all-gather.

deficits.missing: The multi-GPU training loop is presented as a from-scratch DDP bucketing wrapper and (in 3.12) FSDP checkpoint utilities, but there is no single runnable torchrun-launchable train.py that wires model + real dataloader over sharded token files + optimizer + schedule + DDP/FSDP + checkpoint into one script. The pieces exist across chapters and the tooling appendix (accelerate/torchrun/DeepSpeed launch commands) but are never assembled.
deficits.fix: Provide one ~150-line runnable distributed train.py (torchrun entry, init_process_group, shard-aware dataloader, DDP or FSDP wrap, cosine schedule, grad clip, periodic checkpoint via the 3.12 harness) so the 8-GPU path is buildable without the reader integrating five chapters by hand.

deficits.missing: The TinyDDP from-scratch wrapper is runnable and prints loss, but there is no correctness test — the canonical check that DDP gradients/losses match a single-process run on the concatenated batch (bitwise or within fp tolerance) is absent, so a reader cannot confirm their reimplementation is right.
deficits.fix: Add a short verification harness: run the same model/seed/data single-process vs 4-rank TinyDDP, assert torch.allclose on post-sync gradients and on parameters after k optimizer steps; state the expected tolerance (exact match in fp32 SUM order caveats aside).

deficits.missing: TinyDDP silently breaks under gradient accumulation: hooks fire on every backward, so buckets all-reduce after each micro-batch and _ready_counts over-increment across micro-batches. The no_sync() tip covers production DDP but the toy's limitation is not flagged.
deficits.fix: Add one sentence noting the toy fires communication every backward and sketch the two-line fix (a require_sync flag checked in the hook), mirroring DDP's no_sync().

deficits.missing: In compute_mfu(), the attention correction is added as attn_flops = 4 * n_layers * d_model * seq_len (forward-only) while the base term 6P covers forward+backward; the attention term should be 12 * n_layers * d_model * seq_len to be consistent. The adjacent prose formula also carries a stray '/1' ('12 * n_layers * d_model * S / 1') and the recompute paragraph says total FLOPs are 'approximately 4P per token' before correctly concluding 8P.
deficits.fix: Change attn_flops to 12 * n_layers * d_model * seq_len (or state explicitly that the code adds only the forward attention term), remove the '/1' typo, and rewrite the recompute sentence to 'forward 2P + recomputed forward 2P + backward 4P = 8P'.

deficits.missing: The MFU worked numbers are wrong by 2-3x and physically impossible. The compute_mfu example claims 7,000 tok/s/GPU on a 70B model 'typically prints ~42%' — actual value is 149% vs the 1979-TFLOPS peak used (and 297% vs the correct dense bf16 peak of ~989 TFLOPS; 1979 is the 2:4-sparsity figure). The 70B memory-budget example says 'need >= 2.4e6 tokens/s for 50% MFU: ~4,688 tok/s per GPU' — correct is ~1.21e6 tok/s cluster / ~2,356 per GPU against the sparse peak, ~1,178 against dense. 'A well-tuned 70B run achieves 5,000-7,000 tok/s per GPU, corresponding to MFU of 49-66%' is off by ~3x; realistic is ~1,000-2,000 tok/s/GPU.
deficits.fix: Use the dense bf16 H100 peak (989 TFLOPS) for MFU denominators; recompute all worked numbers: 50% MFU on 70B corresponds to ~1,180 tok/s/GPU; state realistic 70B throughput as ~800-1,500 tok/s/GPU at 35-55% MFU; fix the compute_mfu example comment.

deficits.missing: The global-batch-size guidance formula 'GBS ≈ C_critical × S / T where S is sequence length and T is vocabulary size' is meaningless — critical batch size has nothing to do with vocabulary size, and the formula is dimensionally incoherent.
deficits.fix: Replace with the gradient-noise-scale framing (McCandlish et al., B_crit ≈ gradient noise scale) or simply state empirical GBS ranges (1-4M tokens/step for >10B models) and cross-link to the LR/batch-size chapter, which the surrounding prose already gestures at.

deficits.missing: The global-batch-size heuristic 'GBS ≈ C_critical × S / T where T is vocabulary size' is not a recognized relationship (critical batch size does not scale inversely with vocab size) and could mislead a reader sizing a run.
deficits.fix: Delete the formula or replace it with the McCandlish critical-batch-size framing (B_crit from gradient noise scale) plus the empirical 1M-4M tokens/step guidance that already follows.

deficits.missing: ZeRO-3 memory formula given as (2P + 12P)/DP = 14P/DP, omitting the 2P of bf16 gradients; the accompanying text says 'ZeRO-3 shards all 14 bytes'. This contradicts chapter 3.5's correct 16 bytes/param accounting (2 params + 2 grads + 12 optimizer) and the ZeRO paper's (2+2+K)Psi/N with K=12.
deficits.fix: Correct the formula to (2P + 2P + 12P)/DP = 16P/DP and update the surrounding prose and the stage table to be consistent with chapter 3.5.

deficits.missing: Two smaller formula inconsistencies: (a) ZeRO-3 memory is given as (2P+12P)/DP = 14P/DP, omitting the 2P of gradients and contradicting the chapter's own 16-bytes-per-param accounting a few sections later; (b) the interleaved bubble is simplified '(PP-1)/(PP-1+v·m) ≈ 1/(v·m)', dropping the (PP-1) numerator — correct first-order form is (PP-1)/(v·m); (c) the FLOPs/token formula prints a stray '/1' denominator and its attention term (12·L·d·S) disagrees with the code's 4·L·d·S (forward-only vs fwd+bwd).
deficits.fix: Use 16P/DP for ZeRO-3, write the interleaved bubble as (1/v)·(p-1)/(m+p-1) as chapter 3.6 correctly does, remove the '/1', and make the code's attention term 12·L·d·S to match the 6P fwd+bwd convention.

deficits.missing: The SelectiveSSM (Mamba) code crashes when run: in forward(), `y = (h * C[:, t].unsqueeze(2)).sum(-1)` broadcasts (B, N, 1) against h of shape (B, D, N) and raises 'The size of tensor a (D) must match the size of tensor b (N) at non-singleton dimension 1'. Verified by executing the code verbatim (PyTorch 2.9): RuntimeError for any d_model != d_state.
deficits.fix: Change `C[:, t].unsqueeze(2)` to `C[:, t].unsqueeze(1)` so C broadcasts as (B, 1, N) against h (B, D, N), and add a smoke test that actually runs SelectiveSSM/MambaBlock end-to-end with printed expected shapes (the chapter's other blocks have no executed smoke test for this class).

deficits.missing: The Mamba-2 SSD equation is garbled: it states 'Y = (L \circ M) V, L_{ij} = C_i^T h_j (if using state expansion)' with M_{ij} = prod of decays. This conflates the decay mask and the attention-like matrix, and L_{ij} = C_i^T h_j is not a valid entry of either (h_j is a hidden state, not a factor in the mask). The correct SSD form is Y = (L \circ (C B^T)) V where L_{ij} = prod_{k=j+1}^{i} a_k for i >= j (0 otherwise).
deficits.fix: Rewrite the SSD section: define the 1-semiseparable decay mask L_{ij} = prod_{k=j+1}^{i} a_k, then state Y = (L \circ (C B^T)) V, and note this is exactly linear attention with a data-dependent multiplicative causal mask. Keep the block-decomposition remark.

deficits.missing: Sliding-window attention is never explained anywhere in the book — it appears only as name-drops (Mistral 7B and Gemma 2 in reference lists, a flash-attn API knob in 4.2, 'sliding window attention' as an option in the hybrid design-space list). The unit's learning objective 'sliding-window and sparse attention' is unmet: no banded/local causal mask definition, no rolling-buffer KV cache (fixed W-sized cache), no receptive-field-grows-with-depth (~layers x W) argument, no interleaved global/local layer pattern (Gemma 2/3, GPT-oss style).
deficits.fix: Add a 'Sliding-Window and Sparse Attention' section to 2.11 (or 2.10): define the banded causal mask (attend to positions [t-W+1, t]), give the O(N*W) FLOP/memory accounting, explain the rolling-buffer KV cache bounding cache at W tokens, the effective receptive field across L layers, and the interleaved full/local layer recipe with a short mask-construction code snippet (torch or flex_attention mask_mod).

deficits.missing: Sparse attention patterns are essentially absent: no coverage of block-sparse/strided patterns (Sparse Transformer), global+local+random patterns (Longformer/BigBird), or modern trainable sparse attention (DeepSeek NSA, MoBA). Only StreamingLLM/attention sinks (covered well in 2.10) and a LongLoRA citation in 3.13 touch the space.
deficits.fix: In the same new section, add ~1-2 pages on sparse attention: the pattern taxonomy (strided, block-sparse, global tokens), why block granularity matches GPU tiles, and a paragraph on 2025-era learned sparsity (NSA's compressed/selected/sliding branches), with citations. A figure contrasting full / sliding / block-sparse / global+local mask matrices would carry most of the weight.

deficits.missing: The training-time parallel/chunked scan — the actual hardware-aware algorithm that makes Mamba/GLA trainable at speed — is dispatched in one sentence ('production uses parallel scan') and one paragraph on kernel fusion. The provided SelectiveSSM uses a Python for-loop over L, which is correct but does not teach the associative-scan or chunkwise (intra-chunk parallel + inter-chunk recurrent) algorithm that is the SSM counterpart of the FlashAttention story and the heart of this lecture's 'hardware-aware algorithm design' objective.
deficits.fix: Add a section deriving the chunked scan: show the associative combine operator (a1,b1)*(a2,b2)=(a1*a2, a2*b1+b2) for the linear recurrence, and/or the chunkwise GLA/SSD algorithm (process chunks of length C with intra-chunk matmuls, carry an inter-chunk state), with a ~30-line NumPy/PyTorch implementation verified against the sequential loop.

deficits.missing: The linear-attention training code only implements the non-causal parallel form; the comment says 'the causal form requires a cumulative outer-product scan' but never shows it, so a reader cannot train a causal linear-attention LM efficiently from the book alone (the recurrent step is O(N) sequential in Python).
deficits.fix: Add a chunked causal linear attention implementation (chunk-parallel: intra-chunk quadratic masked attention + inter-chunk state passing), ~25 lines, which is also the algorithmic core reused by GLA/RetNet chunkwise forms discussed later in the chapter.

deficits.missing: The RWKVTimeMixing code disagrees with the chapter's own displayed WKV recurrence: the output step uses exp(pp + w - qq)*aa (applying an extra decay w to the historical state), while the displayed equation and the reference BlinkDL kernel use a_{t-1} undecayed (exp(pp - qq)*aa) with decay applied only in the state update. The variant is a u -> u - w reparameterization so it trains, but code and math contradict each other.
deficits.fix: Align the code with the equation: in the output step compute qq = max(pp, kt + u) and use exp(pp - qq) * aa (and same for bb), matching the official RWKV-4 kernel; or add a note explaining the intentional offset.

deficits.missing: Unlike the FlashAttention chapter (which asserts equivalence to a reference and grad-checks the backward), the SSM chapter's code has no numerical checks: linear_attention_parallel is never verified against a recurrent-form rollout, and SelectiveSSM/RWKVTimeMixing have no expected-output or equivalence tests (only a shape print for the hybrid model).
deficits.fix: Add short verification snippets: (a) assert non-causal linear_attention_parallel matches an explicit softmax-free reference and that stepping linear_attention_recurrent_step over t reproduces the causal output; (b) assert the sequential selective scan matches a cumulative-product closed form on a tiny (B=1, L=5, D=2, N=3) example with hand-checkable numbers.

deficits.missing: Dan Fu's signature research line is absent: no mention anywhere in the book of H3, Hyena (implicit long convolutions), FlashFFTConv (hardware-aware O(N log N) FFT convolution), Monarch/butterfly structured matrices, or Based (Taylor linear attention + sliding window). The chapter jumps from S4's FFT-convolution view straight to Mamba.
deficits.fix: Add a short 'Convolutional language models' subsection to 02-transformer/11 covering H3/Hyena long convolutions, the FlashFFTConv idea (Monarch decomposition of the FFT to use tensor cores), and Based's Taylor-feature-map + sliding-window hybrid, with citations.

deficits.missing: The worked FLOP example counts only one of the two matmuls in each block: 'attention FLOPs per layer ~ 2 x 32 x 2048^2 x 128 ~ 34 GFLOPs' is only QK^T (PV doubles it to ~69 GF), and 'feed-forward FLOPs ~ 2 x 2048 x 4096 x 16384 ~ 275 GFLOPs' is only the up-projection (up+down gives ~550 GF). The ~11% ratio survives because both are halved, but the absolute numbers are each understated by 2x. The follow-on sentence 'attention dominates by more than 500x over the MLP at 2048 length' compares attention at 128K against MLP at 2K, which is confusing as written.
deficits.fix: Count both matmuls in each block (attention ~69 GF, FFN ~550 GF at N=2048), and rephrase the 128K comparison to compare attention and MLP at the same sequence length (attention ~8x the MLP at N=131072).

deficits.missing: The RetNet retention equation is written as a scalar-looking double sum '(sum_{m,n} (Q_m K_n^T) gamma^{m-n} 1_{m>=n}) V', which does not typecheck as the N x N masked score matrix it intends.
deficits.fix: Write it as Retention(Q,K,V) = (QK^T \odot D) V with D_{mn} = gamma^{m-n} for m >= n and 0 otherwise.

deficits.missing: In the from-scratch GRPO trainer's rollout(), `model.generate(..., num_return_sequences=G, pad_token_id=eos)` pads shorter completions in a group with EOS up to the group max length, and `resp_mask[i, plen:s.shape[0]] = 1.0` then marks those trailing pad-EOS tokens as response tokens. The loss/ratio/KL are therefore computed on tokens the policy never sampled, injecting advantage-weighted gradient on repeated-EOS positions.
deficits.fix: Truncate the mask at the first EOS in the generated region (e.g. find the first eos_token_id after plen and set mask beyond it to 0), or generate per-sequence lengths from the scores/stopping criteria; add one sentence warning that generate() pads with pad_token_id and masks must stop at the true EOS.

deficits.missing: The worked examples in 5.8 and 5.9 explicitly compute the group std as the population std (divide by G), but the accompanying code uses `g.std(dim=1)` which in PyTorch defaults to the Bessel-corrected sample std (divide by G-1). A reader validating the code against the worked example's expected values (e.g. A1 = +0.992 in 5.8) will get A1 = +0.859 instead (pop std 0.5545 vs sample std 0.6403). Same mismatch in 06-rl-infra/02's grpo_advantages.
deficits.fix: Either pass `correction=0` (unbiased=False) in the code to match the worked examples, or add a one-line note that torch defaults to the sample std, both conventions appear in real codebases (TRL uses the default), and the worked example uses population std — then state the alternative expected values.

deficits.missing: In the rollout() function, resp_mask is set to 1.0 for positions plen..s.shape[0], but model.generate with num_return_sequences pads early-finishing sequences in the group with eos/pad tokens up to the longest completion; those trailing pad tokens are included in the response mask and thus contribute to the policy-gradient loss and KL.
deficits.fix: Mask out tokens after the first EOS in each generated completion (e.g. compute per-sequence generation length via (out[g, plen:] != eos).cumprod or by locating the first eos), or note explicitly that including one EOS is intended and everything after it must be masked.

deficits.missing: The worked numerical example computes the group standard deviation as the population std (divide by G), but the reference code uses torch.Tensor.std(dim=1), which defaults to the Bessel-corrected sample std (divide by G-1). A reader checking the code against the worked numbers will get slightly different advantages (0.5545 vs 0.6403 for the G=4 example).
deficits.fix: Either pass correction=0 (or unbiased=False) in g.std(...) to match the worked example, or add one sentence noting the population/sample-std convention difference and that either is acceptable as long as it is consistent.

deficits.missing: The GRPO chapter's toy driver is commented out and no expected end-to-end training signature is given (e.g. 'mean_reward should climb from ~0.1 to >0.9 within ~100 steps on the toy arithmetic task with Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct'). The verifier code in 5.9 has assert-based tests, but the training loop itself has no expected-values check a reader can use to confirm their implementation converges.
deficits.fix: Add a short 'expected behavior' note after the driver: expected mean-reward trajectory on the toy task, roughly how many steps/minutes on a single consumer GPU, and one or two diagnostic thresholds (fraction of non-degenerate groups, entropy) that indicate a healthy vs. broken run.

deficits.missing: Projector parameter count is stated as ~40M/~42M three times (code comment 'Approx 4096*1024*2 + 4096*4096*2 ≈ 42M', comparison table '~40M', Key Takeaways 'Only ~40M new parameters'). The actual LLaVA-1.5 MLP connector (1024→4096, GELU, 4096→4096) has ~21M params (4.2M + 16.8M); the comment formula erroneously doubles both layers.
deficits.fix: Correct the code comment to '1024*4096 + 4096*4096 ≈ 21M params' and change '~40M' to '~21M' in the Flamingo comparison table and Key Takeaways.

deficits.missing: In the KV-memory worked example, the sentence 'each token costs 2 × 4096 = 8192 bytes (bf16) in the KV cache per layer' is wrong: K + V at d=4096 in bf16 is 2 × 4096 × 2 = 16,384 bytes/token/layer. The displayed equation below it (which includes the ×2 bytes factor) is correct, so the prose contradicts the math it introduces.
deficits.fix: Rewrite the sentence to 'each token costs 2 (K and V) × 4096 dims × 2 bytes = 16,384 bytes per layer' so prose and equation agree.

deficits.missing: The early-fusion vs late-fusion taxonomy is never explicitly defined. The chapter teaches the three connection strategies (projector, cross-attention, unified tokenizer) and 10.5's SOTA box calls Chameleon 'early-fusion', but a reader is never told how these standard terms map onto the architectures.
deficits.fix: Add a short paragraph in 'The Connection Problem' mapping the terminology: late fusion = separate encoder + connector into a frozen-ish LLM (LLaVA/Flamingo); early fusion = single model over a joint token stream from the first layer (Chameleon/Fuyu-style).

deficits.missing: MiniLLaVA always prepends all visual tokens before the text sequence; real LLaVA splices projected image tokens at the position of an <image> placeholder inside the chat template (system prompt precedes the image). The chapter never shows placeholder-based splicing or how labels/attention_mask are rebuilt around the spliced segment, and the caller is required to hand in an attention_mask of shape [B, N_patches+T] that the code never constructs.
deficits.fix: Add ~15 lines implementing <image>-token splicing (find placeholder index, split text embeds, concatenate [pre | visual | post], build attention_mask and -100-padded labels in the same function), noting prepending is a functional simplification.

deficits.missing: Claimed program output is wrong: the chapter states the sanity check with Llama-2-7B dims (d=4096, 32 heads) 'prints approximately 218M parameters per block, consistent with 7B/32 ~ 219M'. The actual code yields ~201.6M (attn 4d^2=67.1M + SwiGLU 3*d*10944=134.5M). Relatedly, the text claims 8*4096/3=10922.7 'rounded to a multiple of 64' gives 11,008, but the code's rounding produces 10,944; Llama-2's 11,008 comes from multiple_of=256. The '7B/32=219M' framing also ignores that 7B includes ~262M of embeddings.
deficits.fix: Correct the printed number to ~202M, fix the rounding discussion (Llama uses multiple_of=256, giving 11,008; the code's 64-multiple gives 10,944), and note that per-block ~202M x 32 + 2x32000x4096 embeddings ~ 6.7B total.

deficits.missing: The stated verification value after the sanity-check code is wrong: 'Running the sanity-check section above with the Llama 2 7B single-block dimensions prints approximately 218M parameters per block ... consistent with 7B/32 ≈ 219M per layer.' The code actually prints ~201.6M (attn 67.1M + SwiGLU with hidden=10944 giving 134.5M + norms). The '7B/32' comparison is also misleading: Llama 2 7B is 6.74B total including two 131M embedding matrices; its true per-block count is ~202M — which the code output actually matches.
deficits.fix: Change the claimed print value to ~202M and rewrite the consistency check as: Llama 2 7B total 6.74B = 32 x ~202M per block + ~0.26B embeddings + final norm. This turns a failing expected value into a correct, stronger verification.

deficits.missing: Misattributed citation for scaled residual initialization: 'Bai et al., Transformers Need Glasses! (2024)' — that paper (Barbero et al.) is about representational collapse / over-squashing at the last token, not initialization variance at depth.
deficits.fix: Cite GPT-2 (Radford et al. 2019) and/or Megatron-LM (Shoeybi et al. 2019) for the 1/sqrt(2L) output-projection scaling, and drop or correctly repurpose the Transformers-Need-Glasses reference.

deficits.missing: InstructionDataset._format has an off-by-one in the loss mask: full_text is tokenized with default add_special_tokens=True (auto-prepends BOS) while the prompt-only encoding uses add_special_tokens=False, so prompt_len undercounts by one and labels[:prompt_len] leaves the LAST prompt token unmasked/supervised. The literal '<s>' in the prompt string plus the auto-added BOS also yields a double-BOS sequence.
deficits.fix: Remove the literal '<s>' from the template, tokenize BOTH full_text and prompt with the same add_special_tokens setting (True), compute prompt_len = prompt_enc['input_ids'].shape[1] under that setting, and add an assertion that tokenizer.decode(input_ids[prompt_len:]) startswith the response. Add a printed token-id/label dump for one example as the chapter's own checklist recommends.

deficits.missing: In InstructionDataset._format, full_text (which begins with a literal '<s>') is tokenized with default add_special_tokens=True while the prompt-only encoding uses add_special_tokens=False. For Llama-2-style tokenizers this produces a duplicate BOS in input_ids and makes prompt_len off by one, leaving the final prompt token unmasked (it receives loss). This is exactly the 'mask boundary' bug class the chapter itself warns about in 'Assistant prefix contamination'.
deficits.fix: Tokenize full_text with add_special_tokens=False as well (the template already contains '<s>'/'</s>'), or drop the literal '<s>'/'</s>' from the template and let the tokenizer add specials consistently for both encodings; add an assert that input_ids[:prompt_len] equals prompt_enc ids.

deficits.missing: Refusal training as a concrete post-training recipe is thin. The book measures refusal/over-refusal well (11.5: XSTest, ASR vs FPR operating points) and covers CAI/RLAIF pipelines (5.11), but nowhere explains how to construct safety SFT/preference data: how many safety examples in the mix, refusal-with-explanation response templates, pairing harmful prompts with borderline-benign contrast prompts to control over-refusal, or safety-specific preference pairs for the reward model. Only a single sentence ('safety refusals... underrepresented in organic data') gestures at it.
deficits.fix: Add a 'Refusal training' section (in 5.1 or 5.11) giving a concrete safety data recipe: target proportion of safety examples in the SFT mix (~1-5%), refusal response format, contrast sets of benign look-alike prompts (XSTest-style) included as comply examples, safety preference-pair construction, and a note on evaluating the result with the 11.5 harness.

deficits.missing: In minimal_harness.py score_choices(), context_ids is encoded with default add_special_tokens=True (which prepends BOS for Llama/Mistral tokenizers) while full_ids is encoded with add_special_tokens=False plus a manually prepended BOS; ctx_len = context_ids.shape[1] + 1 therefore double-counts BOS, so cont_ids = full_ids[0, ctx_len:] skips the first continuation token. Since each continuation (' A'..' D') is typically one token, cont_ids is empty, every choice scores ll=0.0, argmax always returns choice 0, and the harness silently reports ~25% accuracy for exactly the models used in the chapter's own examples (Llama-3, Mistral).
deficits.fix: Encode context with add_special_tokens=False so it matches the full_text encoding (ctx_len = context_ids_no_special.shape[1] + 1 when BOS is manually prepended), add an assertion that len(cont_ids) >= 1 per choice, and add a sanity check in the text (e.g., 'if all four log-likelihoods are identical or zero, your context/continuation split is wrong'). Also warn about the token-boundary hazard of computing ctx_len from a separately-encoded context string.

deficits.missing: mcnemar_test()'s printed example output comment is wrong: for n_01=2, n_10=2 the Yates-corrected statistic is (|2-2|-1)^2/4 = 0.25 with p ~= 0.617, not the claimed {'chi2': 0.0, 'p_value': 1.0}. Additionally the continuity correction (|b-c|-1)^2 is applied without clamping at 0, producing a spurious positive statistic when b == c, and the chi-square approximation is used even for tiny discordant counts (the ch. 11.6 version correctly falls back to the exact binomial test).
deficits.fix: Correct the example output comment, use max(abs(n_01 - n_10) - 1, 0)**2 in the numerator, and add the exact-binomial fallback for n_01 + n_10 < 25 (or simply cross-reference the corrected implementation in 11-evaluation/06-statistical-rigor-eval).

deficits.missing: The minimal harness has no end-to-end verification target: no tiny worked fixture (e.g., a 10-question toy task with a known model where expected acc/acc_norm values are stated), so a reader cannot confirm their implementation is correct — which matters doubly given the score_choices bug would pass unnoticed.
deficits.fix: Add a smoke-test fixture: run the minimal harness on ~20 items with a small public model (e.g., gpt2 or a 0.5B model) and state the expected accuracy range and one hand-checked per-item log-likelihood, plus the invariant checks (choices must have distinct log-likelihoods; null_rate should be reported).

deficits.missing: The decode-bandwidth worked example claims weight traffic per decode step is '2N*b_param = 2*7e9*2 = 2.8e10 bytes = 28 GB' for a 7B bf16 model. Reading N parameters at 2 bytes each is 14 GB, not 28 GB — the '2N' FLOP convention was conflated with bytes. The same chapter correctly states '~14 GB to move every step' in Phase 5, so the text contradicts itself; the derived ~8.4 ms/step and ~8.6 s totals are 2x too high.
deficits.fix: Change the formula to N*b_param bytes (14 GB), recompute: ~4.2 ms per decode step and ~4.3 s over 1024 steps, and adjust the '~12-20 s' downstream estimate accordingly (the qualitative conclusion that generation dominates survives).

deficits.missing: In rl_outer_step(), reward_fn is called with r['response_ids'] (token ids) although every reward function defined in the book takes decoded text; and sync_weights_to_engine is called once with a state dict (from the async sketch) and once with a model, with incompatible signatures. Sketch-level, but a reader stitching the pieces together must notice and fix it.
deficits.fix: Decode the response before scoring (`tok.decode(r['response_ids'], skip_special_tokens=True)`) and unify sync_weights_to_engine to take a state_dict in both call sites.

deficits.missing: The Adam spike-magnitude worked example is internally inconsistent: the spike update uses (1-beta1)=0.1 (beta1=0.9) giving 3e-3, but the 'normal' update applies an extra (1-beta1) factor and reports 3e-6, which requires beta1=0.99. With beta1=0.9 throughout, the normal update is 3e-5 and the spike is ~100x normal, not the claimed '1000x'. The follow-on claim that clipping limits damage 'to ~10x normal' (0.03 vs 3e-6 is 10,000x; vs 3e-5 is 1000x) is also inconsistent.
deficits.fix: Redo the worked example with one consistent beta1=0.9: normal steady-state update ~alpha (m-hat converges to g), spike update ~alpha*(1-beta1)*g/sqrt(v-hat-old); state the ratio correctly (~100x under the fresh-moment assumption) and recompute the with-clipping ratio to match.

deficits.missing: The Q4_K_M internal structure inverts the precision hierarchy: the book says the super-block stores '2 high-precision (6-bit) scales and mins' and 'each sub-block has a 4-bit scale'. In llama.cpp's actual block_q4_K, the super-block scale d and min dmin are fp16, and the 8 sub-block scales/mins are 6-bit (packed in scales[12]); weights are 4-bit.
deficits.fix: Correct to: fp16 super-block scale/min, 6-bit sub-block scales and mins for the 8 sub-blocks of 32 weights, 4-bit quantized weights; cite ggml's block_q4_K struct as the source of truth.

deficits.missing: YaRN attention-temperature formula is stated as 'multiplies the attention logits by a constant 1/t = 0.1 ln(s) + 1'. The YaRN paper defines sqrt(1/t) = 0.1 ln(s) + 1; the scalar mscale = 0.1 ln(s) + 1 is folded into the cos/sin cache (multiplying both q and k), so the logits are effectively scaled by (0.1 ln(s) + 1)^2, not by 0.1 ln(s) + 1. As written, a reader implementing YaRN from this text would use the wrong temperature.
deficits.fix: Correct to: sqrt(1/t) = 0.1 ln(s) + 1, i.e. logits scaled by (0.1 ln s + 1)^2; note that implementations fold mscale = 0.1 ln(s)+1 into the cos/sin tables so the square arises automatically from applying it to both q and k.

deficits.missing: Off-by-one in the 'Minimal Paged Serving Loop': serve_step writes the new token's KV at slot_index(seq_id, cur_len) BEFORE growing the block table, but append_token(seq_id, s['len']) only allocates a new block after the write. When prompt_len (or any current length) is an exact multiple of block_size, slot_index indexes table[cur_len // block_size], one past the last allocated block, and raises IndexError. E.g. prompt_len=32, block_size=16: allocate() gives 2 blocks (positions 0-31); the first decode write at position 32 reads table[2], which does not exist.
deficits.fix: Either call block_mgr.append_token (i.e., grow the table when cur_len % block_size == 0) BEFORE computing slot_index for the write, or make allocate() reserve ceil((num_tokens+1)/block_size) blocks. Add a boundary-case test (prompt length exactly divisible by block_size) to the code listing.

deficits.missing: transfusion_attention_mask() never grants attention for j > i, even within the same image block ('if j > i: pass'), so the produced mask is purely causal. This directly contradicts its own docstring ('all image positions in the same block (bidirectional within-image)') and Transfusion's defining design of bidirectional attention inside image blocks. A reader copying this code trains with the wrong mask.
deficits.fix: Fix the loop so image position i also attends to j > i when image_block[j] == image_block[i], and make the 'Quick test' assert the bidirectional property (e.g., assert mask[3,6] and mask[6,3] for a 4-token image block) instead of just printing the mask.

deficits.missing: The IsoFLOP method (Chinchilla approach 2) — the method the deliverable is named after — gets exactly one prose sentence ('fits a parabola in log N to find the valley... then fits a power law through those valleys') with no code, no worked example, and no discussion of how many points per slice, quadratic-fit robustness, or extracting D_opt from each valley. Only the parametric fit (approach 3) is fully implemented.
deficits.fix: Add a runnable IsoFLOP section: group synthetic runs into 4-6 fixed-C slices, fit a quadratic in log N per slice (np.polyfit), extract N_opt(C) and D_opt(C)=C/(6*N_opt), fit log N_opt vs log C to recover exponent a, and cross-check a against the parametric fit's beta/(alpha+beta). Include the expected recovered exponent as a printed check.

deficits.missing: No concrete procedure for DESIGNING a sweep under a constrained experimentation budget. The chapter says 'run a grid of small models at varying N and D' and lists pitfalls (unconverged runs, LR schedule matched to token count), but never answers: given a sweep budget of, say, 1-2% of the final run's FLOPs, how many compute slices, how many models per slice, how to space N around the expected optimum, how to set per-run LR/batch (linking the ch. 3.10 scaling rules), and how the run-table FLOPs sum to the budget.
deficits.fix: Add a worked sweep-design example: 'You have 2e18 FLOPs for experiments before a 1e21 run.' Produce an explicit run table (C slice, N values, D values, LR, batch, per-run FLOPs, total) using log-spaced slices and ~6 models per slice spanning ~1 order of magnitude in N, with LR chosen via muP or the batch-scaling rules, and total cost verified against the budget. This is the exact skill the assignment demands.

deficits.missing: The text claims 'The tokens_per_param column comes out roughly constant across the four budgets (it drifts only because alpha != beta exactly)'. Running the chapter's own chinchilla_optimal code with the given constants (A=406.4, B=410.7, alpha=0.34, beta=0.28) prints tok/param = 32.1, 50.1, 78.2, 122.1 for C = 1e19..1e25 — a 4x drift that is never near 20. D/N scales as C^{(alpha-beta)/(alpha+beta)} = C^0.097, i.e. x1.25 per decade of compute, which is not 'roughly constant' over 6 decades.
deficits.fix: Either (a) demonstrate constancy with alpha = beta (e.g. both 0.34), noting that Chinchilla's Approaches 1-2 fitted a ~ b ~ 0.5, and separately show the Approach-3 constants produce a slow C^0.1 drift; or (b) print the actual expected output in the text and rewrite the sentence to say the ratio drifts by ~25% per decade of compute and exceeds 20 tok/param at frontier budgets under the Approach-3 fit (a known property of that fit). Show the expected numeric output of the loop either way.

deficits.missing: The 'Fitting a Scaling Law From Scratch' demo has no stated expected values or tolerance, and when actually run it recovers A=878 vs ground-truth 406 and E=1.83 vs 1.69 (from just 2% noise on 42 points), overpredicting extrapolated loss at C=1e25 by ~0.1 nats. The text never mentions this identifiability problem (E/A/alpha are strongly correlated), so a reader who runs the code sees a 2x parameter mismatch with no way to know whether it is a bug or expected behavior. There is also no quantitative uncertainty treatment (bootstrap/Hessian confidence intervals on alpha, beta, E; prediction intervals on extrapolated loss), despite 'fit diagnostics and confidence' being a core objective.
deficits.fix: Add a paragraph after the fit acknowledging the parameter-correlation/identifiability issue with expected recovery ranges, then add ~20 lines of bootstrap resampling (refit on resampled grids, report percentile CIs on exponents and a fan of extrapolated-loss predictions). Note the compensating-error phenomenon the demo exhibits: the allocation N*(C) extrapolates far better than the loss L*(C) because the loss surface is flat near the optimum. Cite the Epoch AI Chinchilla replication ('Chinchilla Scaling: A replication attempt', Besiroglu et al. 2024) as the real-world case where the original fit's parametric estimates were contested.

deficits.missing: The emergent-abilities demo defines smooth_per_token_accuracy(N) = 1 - 0.9*(N/1e9)**-0.12, which is NEGATIVE for N < ~4.2e8; since Ns = np.logspace(8, 12, 9), the first two printed rows show per-token 'accuracy' of about -0.19 and -0.03 (only exact_match clips). Verified numerically.
deficits.fix: Clip inside smooth_per_token_accuracy (np.clip(..., 0, 1)) or start Ns at 1e9 so the printed table shows valid probabilities.

deficits.missing: predict_log_loss's comment claims 'log-sum-exp form keeps it numerically stable', but the code exponentiates each term and sums directly — no logsumexp is used. Harmless at these magnitudes but the comment misdescribes the code and misattributes Chinchilla's actual LSE trick.
deficits.fix: Either use scipy.special.logsumexp over the stacked terms (matching Hoffmann et al.'s actual objective) or correct the comment to say the parameterization via logs keeps the fitted constants positive.

deficits.missing: No end-of-chapter exercises rehearsing the full assignment loop (design sweep -> fit both methods -> project N*, D* at 10x budget), and component-to-LOSS attribution (e.g., how loss responds to aspect ratio, head count, batch/LR misconfiguration at fixed FLOPs) is only qualitative, spread across 02-transformer/10 and 03-pretraining/10.
deficits.fix: Add 2-3 exercises: (a) hold out the largest synthetic run, fit on the rest, verify extrapolated loss within 2%; (b) refit with under-converged runs deliberately included and observe the Kaplan-style bias in the exponents; (c) given a fixed C slice, predict the loss penalty of training at 2x and 0.5x the optimal N.

deficits.missing: Expected loss/perplexity-vs-tokens milestones for a real BPE run are absent. build-gpt states the toy expectation (first loss ~= ln(65)=4.17, <1.5 within a couple thousand steps) and the objective chapter gives the init sanity check (loss ~= ln V), but there is no 'a compute-optimal 125M model on ~2.5B tokens should reach ~X nats/token / BPB ~Y' checkpoint a reader can validate a real run against.
deficits.fix: Add 2-3 concrete (N, D, expected held-out nats/token and BPB) reference points for small models (e.g. 125M and 1B at Chinchilla-optimal tokens) so a reader can confirm their real run is on-curve, not just their toy run.

deficits.missing: IsoFLOP profiles are an explicit learning objective but receive only two sentences of prose ('runs many models at each of several fixed compute budgets, then fits a parabola in log N to find the valley... finally fits a power law through those valleys'). There is no implementation of Chinchilla Approach 2, no worked example of the parabola fit in log N, and no guidance on grid design (how many N values per budget, how wide a range). Only Approach 3 (parametric fit) is implemented.
deficits.fix: Add a ~30-line code block implementing the IsoFLOP method on the same synthetic grid: for each of 4-5 fixed C budgets, sweep N (with D = C/6N), fit a quadratic in log N to the losses, extract the valley N*(C), then fit log N* vs log C to recover the exponent a; show it agrees with the parametric fit's beta/(alpha+beta). Populate the {{fig:scaling-kaplan-vs-chinchilla}} placeholder with an isoFLOP-valley illustration.

deficits.missing: The fitting code's expected recovery quality is not stated. Running it as written recovers exponents well (alpha 0.390 vs true 0.340, beta 0.290 vs 0.280) but E and A poorly (E 1.826 vs 1.69, A 878.5 vs 406.0) due to ill-conditioning of the additive power-law fit at 2% noise. A reader who runs the code and sees A off by 2x has no way to know whether their run succeeded.
deficits.fix: Print/state the expected recovered values for the fixed seed, and add a sentence explaining that E/A/B are weakly identified while the exponents (which determine the allocation) recover much more tightly — and that the right accuracy check is the implied allocation exponent beta/(alpha+beta) and the extrapolated loss, not the raw constants.

deficits.missing: Chinchilla's IsoFLOP-profile method (method 2) is described in one prose bullet but never implemented, even though it is the most practical method for a small sweep and the most robust to misspecification of the parametric form.
deficits.fix: Add ~30 lines: at each of several fixed compute budgets, fit a parabola to loss vs log(N), extract the valley N_opt(C), then fit log N_opt vs log C by least squares to get the exponent a directly. Show it agrees with the parametric fit's beta/(alpha+beta).

deficits.missing: Comment in predict_log_loss claims 'log-sum-exp form keeps it numerically stable', but the code simply exponentiates each term and sums (np.exp(terms[0]) + np.exp(terms[1]) + np.exp(terms[2])) — no max-subtraction, no scipy.special.logsumexp. It is not an LSE implementation and provides no stability benefit over computing the loss directly.
deficits.fix: Either use scipy.special.logsumexp(terms, axis=0) as Chinchilla's Appendix actually does, or delete the misleading comment.

deficits.missing: Guidance on designing the real small-model sweep is thin: how many runs, what N/D spread (orders of magnitude needed), that each run needs its cosine decay matched to its own token endpoint (stated as Kaplan's bug but not turned into a sweep-design checklist), and how to hold out the largest run for extrapolation validation in code rather than prose.
deficits.fix: Add a short 'designing your sweep' checklist (>=2 orders of magnitude in N, >=5 points per axis, per-run LR decay to endpoint, fixed data distribution and tokenizer across runs, several seeds at the smallest scale to estimate noise) and a 5-line code cell that refits with the largest-N runs held out and reports prediction error on them.

deficits.missing: No complete end-to-end byte-level BPE tokenizer. The trainer (train_bpe) is character-level with a '</w>' end-of-word marker; the bytes_to_unicode map is given separately, but they are never assembled: there is no vocab-to-integer-ID table, no encode(str)->List[int], no decode(List[int])->str implementation (decode is prose only), no special-token handling in encode/decode, and the GPT-2 pre-tokenization regex is described but never shown. The '</w>' convention conflicts with the byte-level 'Ġ' convention and the reader is not told to drop it when going byte-level.
deficits.fix: Add a complete `ByteLevelBPETokenizer` class: train on raw text via regex pre-tokenization (print the actual GPT-2/cl100k regex) over byte-mapped symbols without '</w>', build merges + vocab->ID table, implement encode()->List[int] and decode()->str (inverting bytes_to_unicode, bytes.decode(errors='replace')), handle special tokens, and verify with a round-trip test on a unicode string plus a comparison against tiktoken('gpt2') or minbpe output on a fixed input.

deficits.missing: The named 'tokenizer round-trip' test is absent as code. The chapter asserts the GPT-2 bytes-to-unicode map (b2u[32]=='G-dot', 256 distinct chars) and states in prose that byte-level BPE decodes losslessly, but there is no decode() implementation for the from-scratch BPE, no explicit `assert decode(encode(s)) == s` test over adversarial inputs (multi-byte unicode, emoji, invalid UTF-8, leading spaces, special tokens), and no cross-validation of the from-scratch encoder against a reference (tiktoken/HF) on identical text.
deficits.fix: Add a decode() for the byte-level BPE (ids -> merged symbols -> b2u-inverse -> bytes -> utf-8 with errors='replace'), a round-trip test loop over an adversarial string list, and a short harness that encodes the same corpus with the from-scratch tokenizer and tiktoken gpt2 and asserts identical token counts/ids when using GPT-2's merges.

deficits.missing: No end-to-end byte-level BPE tokenizer. The runnable trainer (train_bpe/encode_word) is character-level with a '</w>' end-of-word marker, while the byte-level section provides only the bytes_to_unicode map. The glue -- pre-tokenize into chunks, map UTF-8 bytes through the table, train WITHOUT '</w>' (GPT-2 does not use it), assign integer IDs, and decode ids -> printable symbols -> bytes -> UTF-8 text -- is described in one prose sentence but never shown as code. The reader must reconcile two incompatible schemes ('</w>' marker vs. byte-level leading-space chunks) on their own.
deficits.fix: Add a ~60-line ByteLevelBPETokenizer class combining the existing pieces: train(corpus) using pre-tokenized byte-mapped chunks (no '</w>'), encode(text) -> list[int], decode(ids) -> str (invert b2u, bytes(...).decode('utf-8', errors='replace')), with an explicit ID convention (256 base byte tokens + one ID per merge in learned order) and a round-trip assert: decode(encode(s)) == s on a string containing emoji, CJK, and leading spaces.

deficits.missing: The GPT-2/GPT-4 pre-tokenization regex is repeatedly invoked ('BPE merges only within a chunk', 'why cl100k keeps digits in groups of at most three') but the actual pattern is never given, so a reader cannot reproduce the merge-boundary behavior of a real byte-level tokenizer, nor handle whitespace attachment (the ' hello' single-token behavior the chapter itself highlights).
deficits.fix: Print the actual GPT-2 pattern ('s|'t|'re|'ve|'m|'ll|'d| ?\p{L}+| ?\p{N}+| ?[^\s\p{L}\p{N}]+|\s+(?!\S)|\s+) and the cl100k digit-group change, explain each alternation in one line, and show it feeding chunks into the BPE trainer.

deficits.missing: Line ~248 claims 'GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4, Llama, Mistral ... use byte-level BPE', but line ~366 (and the comparison table) says Llama and early Mistral use SentencePiece. In fact Llama-1/2 and early Mistral use SentencePiece BPE with byte-fallback (not GPT-2-style byte-level BPE); only Llama-3 switched to a tiktoken-style byte-level tokenizer. The two passages contradict each other.
deficits.fix: State the lineage precisely: Llama-1/2 and Mistral-v0.x = SentencePiece BPE (32k, byte-fallback, '▁' marker); Llama-3 = tiktoken-style byte-level BPE (128k). Remove Llama/Mistral from the blanket byte-level claim.

deficits.missing: Claim that 'Llama models split numbers into individual digits' is only true of Llama-1/2; Llama-3 tokenizes digits in groups of up to three (like cl100k). Stated unqualified, a reader inspecting Llama-3 tokens would find the book wrong.
deficits.fix: Qualify: Llama-1/2 and PaLM split per digit; Llama-3/GPT-4 group digits 1-3 at a time via the pre-tokenizer regex.

deficits.missing: No verification against a reference implementation for the byte-level deliverable: the chapter never shows checking the from-scratch encoder's output token-by-token against tiktoken/minbpe on the same merge table, and gives no expected vocab/merge counts for a small real corpus (e.g. train 500 merges on a few MB and report bytes-per-token).
deficits.fix: Add a short 'verify your tokenizer' block: load GPT-2's vocab.json/merges.txt into the from-scratch encoder and assert equality with tiktoken's gpt2 encoding on a test paragraph; report expected bytes/token (~4.0 for English at 50k vocab).

deficits.missing: Training-at-scale cost is not treated: the shown trainer recounts every pair from scratch each merge (O(corpus) per merge). Real trainers (HF tokenizers, minbpe on large corpora, CS336 assignment 1) update pair counts incrementally and parallelize pre-tokenization; nothing tells the reader what to expect when training 32k merges on gigabytes.
deficits.fix: Add a paragraph on incremental pair-count updates (only pairs adjacent to a merge site change), unique-word compression of the corpus, and a rough wall-clock estimate (naive vs incremental) for a 1 GB corpus at 32k merges.

deficits.missing: There is no single coherent chapter that stitches the full real-scale pipeline (train a BPE tokenizer on a corpus -> clean/dedup -> pack into shards -> instantiate a real 125M/1B GPT -> multi-GPU launch -> checkpoint -> eval perplexity -> sample) into one integrated, followable walkthrough. build-gpt (2.7) is a complete coherent path ONLY at toy char-level/laptop scale; at real scale the reader must themselves assemble ~16 separate Part III chapters plus the tooling appendix. The unit's core demand ('a single coherent path from raw text to a trained, sampled-from model') is met only for Tiny-Shakespeare, not for a GPU/BPE run.
deficits.fix: Add a capstone recipe chapter that runs one concrete model (e.g. 125M, GPT-2-ish, BPE vocab) end to end: exact commands to train/apply a BPE tokenizer, build tokenized .bin shards (the nanoGPT prepare.py pattern), the model config, a torchrun/FSDP launch, a checkpoint+resume, a periodic val-perplexity eval, and a final sample. Cross-link each step back to its deep-dive chapter.

deficits.missing: No per-scale config+timing table spanning laptop / 1 GPU / 8 GPU / multi-node for one concrete model. Ch 3.10 gives an hparam table (125M/1B/7B/70B/400B: peak LR, warmup, batch tokens, weight decay) but not throughput or wall-clock; ch 3.4 gives a single multi-node budget worked example (256 GPUs, 21 days, MFU 0.45); build-gpt gives only the laptop config. A reader cannot read off 'on 8xA100 this exact 1B config trains at ~X tok/s and finishes 20B tokens in ~Y hours.'
deficits.fix: Add a table with columns [scale, GPUs, model config, effective batch tokens, expected MFU, tokens/sec, wall-clock for the target token budget, GPU-hours/$] for laptop, 1 GPU, 8 GPU, and multi-node, using the 6ND + MFU accounting already derived in 3.4.

deficits.missing: TP coverage omits the vocab-parallel embedding and the parallel (vocab-sharded) cross-entropy loss — the embedding and LM head are the largest single matrices in the model, and an end-to-end TP transformer cannot be assembled without sharding them (Megatron's VocabParallelEmbedding masking + all-reduce trick, and the max-subtraction + two-all-reduce parallel softmax cross-entropy).
deficits.fix: Add a short section with code for VocabParallelEmbedding (mask out-of-range token ids, zero their rows, all-reduce) and vocab-parallel cross-entropy (local max, all-reduce max, local sum-exp, all-reduce sum, gather target logit), with exact shapes.

deficits.missing: The expert-parallel MoE forward sketch calls undefined helpers (bucket_tokens_by_rank, run_local_experts, weighted_scatter_add) and uses a global ep_group; the all-to-all recv buffer sized with torch.empty_like(send_buf) silently assumes perfectly equal per-rank counts, which is exactly the imbalanced case the surrounding prose warns about.
deficits.fix: Either label the count-exchange step explicitly (a preliminary all_to_all of per-rank token counts to size recv buffers, as real implementations do) or provide the ~15-line bucket_tokens_by_rank helper, so the sketch does not paper over the hardest part of EP.

deficits.missing: Prose swaps FlashAttention's standard notation: 'the running max ℓ and running sum m' — ℓ is the running sum and m the running max, and the chapter's own code uses m_run as the max and l_run as the sum, contradicting the sentence.
deficits.fix: Change to 'the running max m and running sum ℓ' to match both the FlashAttention papers and the code below it.

deficits.missing: The expert-parallel MoE sketch relies on undefined helpers (bucket_tokens_by_rank, weighted_scatter_add, run_local_experts) and uses torch.empty_like(send_buf)/empty_like(local_out) for all_to_all_single, which silently assumes equal per-rank token counts — the variable-split-size handling (input_split_sizes/output_split_sizes from an exchanged counts tensor) is the actual hard part of EP.
deficits.fix: Show the counts exchange (all_to_all on a per-rank count tensor) and pass explicit input/output split sizes to all_to_all_single, or explicitly defer to the MoE chapter if a complete implementation lives there.

deficits.missing: No correctness-verification recipe for any of the parallelism schemes: nothing tells the reader to check that a TP=2 (or PP=2, CP=2) run reproduces the TP=1 loss/logits bit-for-bit (up to reduction order) on 2 GPUs before scaling up — the standard sanity harness for distributed implementations.
deficits.fix: Add a short 'verify before you scale' box: fixed seed, tiny model, compare single-GPU logits/loss vs TP=2/PP=2/CP=2 with torch.allclose tolerances, plus the KV-head divisibility and stage-balance checks as assertions.

deficits.missing: The capstone trains only the GPT-2-style variant (LayerNorm, GELU, learned absolute positions). The assignment's modern stack (RoPE + RMSNorm + SwiGLU causal MHA + weight tying) is never assembled into one trained model: 2.5 implements apply_rope, 2.6 implements RMSNorm/SwiGLU and a 'RoPE-ready' block, but 2.6's MinimalMHA never actually threads the cos/sin caches through its forward, and 2.7 uses wpe. The reader must do the RoPE-into-MHA integration (cache construction per layer, applying after the QKV reshape, handling positions during incremental generation) without a worked reference.
deficits.fix: Add a 'Modern GPT' section (or appendix) to 2.7 that swaps in RMSNorm, SwiGLUFFN, and RoPE'd attention: build cos/sin once in the model, pass into each block, apply after the (B,nh,T,hd) reshape, keep weight tying, and show the same Tiny Shakespeare run reaching an equal-or-better loss so the reader can verify the integration.

deficits.missing: The training loop has no checkpoint save/resume; 3.12's code is FSDP/DCP-oriented and heavy for the single-GPU assignment scale. A reader building the A1 deliverable ('training loop with checkpointing') must invent the minimal torch.save/load pattern (model + optimizer + step + RNG state) themselves.
deficits.fix: Add ~15 lines to the 2.7 loop: periodic torch.save of {model_state, optimizer_state, iter_num, config, rng_states} and a resume path that restores all four, with a note that 3.12 covers the distributed version; demonstrate loss continuity across a kill/resume.

deficits.missing: HuggingFace transformers is used pervasively as an API (AutoModel, Trainer, chat templates) but its internals are never mapped: no pointer to modeling_llama.py, no explanation of how the from-scratch GPT of this chapter corresponds to a transformers modeling file, attention backends selection (attn_implementation), or generate()'s structure.
deficits.fix: Add a closing section 'Reading the real thing' that maps this chapter's from-scratch modules onto transformers/models/llama/modeling_llama.py (LlamaAttention, LlamaMLP, LlamaRMSNorm, rotary embedding, GenerationMixin.generate), so readers can navigate the library source the way the vLLM/Megatron chapters already enable.

deficits.missing: The activation-memory rule of thumb is stated as 'M_act ~ 12 x B x T x d x L bytes (fp16)' but the LLaMA-7B worked example then multiplies by an additional 2 bytes/element (12 x 1 x 2048 x 4096 x 32 x 2). The formula and the example disagree by exactly 2x on the deliverable quantity (the memory budget); a reader cannot tell whether 12BTdL is elements or bytes.
deficits.fix: State the formula in ELEMENTS (M_act ~ 12*B*T*d*L elements) and multiply by bytes-per-element for the chosen dtype explicitly in both the formula and the example; note the coefficient's relation to the Megatron 34*s*b*h + 5*a*s^2*b bytes formula and when the T^2 score term is absent (FlashAttention).

deficits.missing: Checkpointing worked example: 'sqrt(L) x (one layer's activations) ~ 0.2 GB' — with L=32 and ~0.2 GB/layer this is sqrt(32)*0.2 ~ 1.1 GB, not 0.2 GB (the answer 112.2 GB total is therefore ~113 GB).
deficits.fix: Correct the arithmetic to ~1.1 GB (or state the store-only-layer-inputs variant, L*B*T*d*2B ~ 0.5 GB, and label which strategy is being computed).

deficits.missing: QLoRA worked example: 'M_LoRA = 2 x 2 x 16 x 4096 x 32 ~ 0.27 GB' — the printed expression evaluates to ~8.4M (i.e., ~8.4 MB if bytes), not 0.27 GB; a realistic rank-16 adapter set over all linear layers of a 7B model is ~40M params ~ 80 MB bf16. The subsequent optimizer-state line inherits the wrong base.
