deficits.fix: Recompute with an explicit per-matrix formula r*(d_in+d_out) x (number of adapted matrices per layer) x L x bytes, and carry the corrected base into the optimizer-state estimate.

deficits.missing: evaluate_mc_batch()'s shuffle_seed docstring claims 'randomly shuffles answer order to test position bias', but the code shuffles the zipped (prediction, gold) pairs jointly — a no-op on accuracy that shuffles example order, not answer-option order. It cannot detect position bias; a reader using it to test position bias will conclude their model has none.
deficits.fix: Either remove the shuffle_seed parameter or implement true option-order permutation: re-render the question with permuted choices, re-run the model, and remap gold labels through the permutation before comparing. Testing position bias requires new model calls, not post-hoc relabeling — say so explicitly.

deficits.missing: Factual slips in the benchmark survey: MBPP is stated as '374 crowd-sourced Python problems' (MBPP has 974 problems; the commonly-evaluated test split is 500 and the sanitized subset 427); the MATH categories list includes 'calculus' and 'combinatorics' (actual categories are Prealgebra, Algebra, Intermediate Algebra, Counting & Probability, Geometry, Number Theory, Precalculus); and 'a 7B model scores 5% on GPQA, that is near random chance (25%)' — 5% is far below chance, which itself signals a broken answer extractor rather than floor performance.
deficits.fix: Correct the MBPP count (974 total / 500 test split), fix the MATH category list, and rewrite the GPQA floor-effect example so a below-chance score is diagnosed as an extraction/format failure.

deficits.missing: Contamination detection covers n-gram overlap with working code, but the stronger methods (memorization/completion probes, canary strings, paraphrase/shuffle behavioral tests) are described in prose only, and the deliverable is a 'contamination-aware' harness. The 'MeMo and DataComp lines of work' citation for memorization probes is also dubious (DataComp is a dataset-curation benchmark, not a memorization-probe method).
deficits.fix: Add a short runnable completion-probe implementation (feed first half of each benchmark item, measure verbatim/near-verbatim continuation rate via longest-common-substring or edit distance against the second half, with a decision threshold), and replace the MeMo/DataComp citation with accurate references (e.g., Carlini et al. extraction/memorization work, Oren et al. exchangeability test, Golchin & Surdeanu guided prompting).

deficits.missing: The ring_attention_forward pseudocode produces NaN for causal=True: when a received KV chunk lies entirely in the future of a query row (e.g. rank 0 receives rank N-1's chunk at step 1), the entire score row is set to -inf, so torch.softmax returns NaN and the NaN propagates into output_acc even though exp_new=0 (NaN*0=NaN).
deficits.fix: Skip fully-masked KV blocks (if kv_global_start > q_global_start + T_local - 1: rotate and continue) or guard the softmax/merge against all--inf rows; note that production kernels skip these blocks entirely, which is also the source of the causal load imbalance discussed in 3.6.

deficits.missing: Formal mathematics -- one of the three named objectives of this guest lecture -- is essentially absent from the book. There is no coverage of interactive theorem proving (Lean/Isabelle/Coq), autoformalization, prover models (AlphaProof, AlphaGeometry, DeepSeek-Prover, Minerva-style math specialists), or the miniF2F/PutnamBench benchmarks. Grep across all content finds only two passing mentions of FrontierMath as a benchmark and one sentence about 'formal mathematics' in 11-evaluation/01-eval-landscape. This is a conceptual gap that matters for the book's own thesis: a proof checker is the limiting case of a verifiable reward (zero false positives), which is exactly why formal math is a frontier RLVR domain.
deficits.fix: Add a section 'Formal Mathematics: Proof Assistants as Perfect Verifiers' to 5.9 (or an aside in 5.10): what a proof assistant (Lean 4) is, the autoformalization problem (natural-language math -> formal statements), how prover models are trained with the type-checker as the reward signal (expert iteration / AlphaProof-style RL), the miniF2F and PutnamBench benchmarks, and why formal verification sidesteps reward hacking entirely. 800-1200 words plus a short Lean proof example would suffice; no full implementation is required for a lecture unit.

deficits.missing: Gradient clipping is given as the correct formula g <- g*min(1, c/||g||) but the code delegates to nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_; a from-scratch implementation (global norm across all params, then scale) is never shown, though CS336 A1 requires implementing it.
deficits.fix: Add a 6-line from-scratch clip_grad_norm (sqrt of sum of per-param grad norm squares, in-place scale if above threshold) verified against the torch utility.

deficits.missing: build_mup_optimizer puts all MuPLinear parameters (weights AND biases) into the width-scaled LR group (lr = base_lr * proxy_width/actual_width). Under muP with Adam, bias-like (vector) parameters should get a width-invariant LR; scaling bias LR down by 1/width departs from the muP prescription.
deficits.fix: Partition within each MuPLinear: matrix weight gets the 1/width-scaled LR, bias goes to the width-invariant group. One extra condition on param.ndim.

deficits.missing: The muP section gives no way to verify a muP implementation is correct. The standard check — the coordinate check (plot per-layer activation/update scale vs width; curves should be flat across widths under muP and blow up/shrink under SP) — is only alluded to via the microsoft/mup repo description.
deficits.fix: Add a ~25-line coordinate-check: instantiate the model at widths 256/512/1024/2048, take a few Adam steps on fixed data, log mean |activation| per layer, and state the expected result (flat in width for muP, growing for SP). This is the test that catches the majority of real muP bugs.

deficits.missing: Worked example states 'For a 7B model with hidden dim d=4096, muP-optimal LR scales as 1/d' — this conflates the two parameterizations. Under muP the tuned base LR is width-invariant (that is the entire point); it is the per-layer effective LR of hidden matrices that carries the 1/d factor, and under SP the optimal global LR shrinks roughly with width.
deficits.fix: Reword to: 'under standard parameterization the optimal LR shrinks with width (roughly 1/d for hidden matrices); at d=4096 published peak LRs fall in 1e-4..3e-3', and reserve 'muP' for the width-invariant-base-LR claim.

deficits.missing: muP transfer scope is not delimited: muP guarantees transfer across WIDTH only; transfer across depth, batch size, and training horizon is empirical and weaker (depth-muP / complete-P are separate lines of work). A reader could wrongly assume a width-256 sweep transfers to a model that is also 4x deeper and trained 100x longer.
deficits.fix: Add a short caveat box: what muP does and does not guarantee (width: yes; depth: approximate, cite Yang et al. depth-muP; horizon and batch size: retune or use the batch-size scaling rules from this same chapter).

deficits.missing: Two blemishes: (1) LayerNorm is written as (x-mu)/(sigma+eps) with eps added to the std outside the square root, inconsistent with the correct form sqrt(sigma^2+eps) given in ch 2.6 and used by PyTorch. (2) The parameter-count worked example uses a GQA config labelled 'similar to Llama 3 8B' but concludes it is 'close to the marketed 7B' of Llama 2 and attributes the 5.9B-vs-7B gap to vocab size — the real reason is that Llama 2 7B uses full MHA (no GQA), so its attention block is 67M not 42M per layer.
deficits.fix: Fix the LayerNorm epsilon placement, and either recompute the worked example with Llama 2 7B's actual MHA config (recovering ~6.7B) or consistently frame it as Llama 3 8B (128K vocab, GQA) so the totals reconcile.

deficits.missing: The depth/width section gives an L/d table and the head_dim=128 convention, but never walks the reader forward through the full sizing procedure: given a target parameter budget N, derive d, L, n_heads, n_kv_heads, d_ff, and vocab size step by step.
deficits.fix: Add a short worked exercise: 'You have a 3B-parameter budget; specify every config field of a Llama-style model and verify the count' — this directly rehearses the unit's deliverable end-to-end in one place.

deficits.missing: The occupancy worked example contains an uncorrected drafting artifact in the published text: '5 blocks -> 5x256=1280 threads = 20 warps... wait, 5 blocks of 256 = 1280 threads = 40 warps'. The final figure (40 warps, 62.5%) is correct, but the 'wait' self-correction should not appear in a textbook.
deficits.fix: Delete the '= 20 warps... wait,' fragment and state directly: '5 blocks x 256 threads = 1,280 threads = 40 warps = 62.5% occupancy.'

deficits.missing: TPU learning objective is fully covered but lives entirely in 1.10; chapter 1.8 (the flagship hardware chapter) never forward-references the systolic-array contrast, so a reader auditing 'GPUs, TPUs' from 1.8 alone will miss it. Also, Grace Hopper NVLink-C2C is mentioned but PCIe generation labels (Gen4 vs Gen5) behind the '~32-64 GB/s' figure are not stated, while 1.9 quotes 'PCIe Gen 5 ~128 GB/s bidirectional' - consistent only if the reader realizes 1.8's number is unidirectional.
deficits.fix: Add one sentence in 1.8's memory-hierarchy table or NVLink section labeling the PCIe figure ('PCIe Gen5 x16: ~64 GB/s per direction, ~128 GB/s bidirectional') and a one-line pointer to 1.10 for the systolic-array/TPU contrast.

deficits.missing: In the reduce-scatter demo, each rank contributes torch.full((world_size,), float(rank)), so every rank's output is sum(0..world_size-1) = world_size*(world_size-1)/2 (identical on all ranks). The code comment claims 'Rank i's output = sum of input[i] from all ranks = rank * world_size', which is wrong on both the value and the rank-dependence.
deficits.fix: Change the comment to '= sum over ranks r of r = world_size*(world_size-1)/2, same on every rank', or change the input to torch.arange(world_size)+rank so the per-rank outputs actually differ and illustrate the scatter.

deficits.missing: The Interview Corner remedy claims switching to 'ZeRO Stage 1 or 2 (reduce-scatter then all-gather) ... reduces peak communication by the same ~2x factor'. Total ZeRO-1/2 communication volume equals DDP's all-reduce (reduce-scatter + all-gather = all-reduce), as chapter 3.5 itself correctly states ('Communication volume is essentially the same as DDP'). Presenting ZeRO-1/2 as the top remedy for a communication-bound job is misleading.
deficits.fix: Rephrase: ZeRO-1/2 fixes memory redundancy at roughly the same communication volume; the actual communication remedies are shrinking the DP world via TP within node, gradient compression, hierarchical/hybrid sharding, and larger batch/accumulation.

deficits.missing: KTO formula and code apply beta twice: r is defined as beta*log(pi/pi_ref) (the implicit reward already includes beta), then the value function applies sigmoid(beta*(r - z0)) again. In the KTO paper the beta appears once, on the un-scaled log-ratio inside the sigmoid.
deficits.fix: Either define r as the raw log-ratio and keep sigma(beta*(r - z0)), or keep r = beta*log-ratio and use sigma(r - z0); make the math block and kto_loss code consistent.

deficits.missing: orpo_loss docstring says the odds computation requires LENGTH-NORMALIZED (per-token mean) log-probs, but the function signature accepts 'SEQUENCE log-probs (sum over tokens)' and never normalizes; a reader passing summed log-probs gets log1p(-exp(logp)) ~ 0 and the odds term degenerates to a plain log-prob difference.
deficits.fix: Normalize inside the function (divide by response token counts passed as arguments) or change the docstring and parameter names to demand per-token mean log-probs explicitly.

deficits.missing: Claim that <|im_start|> is 'short for image marker in the original GPT-4-V vocabulary' is false: ChatML and the im_start/im_end tokens predate GPT-4V (they shipped with gpt-3.5-turbo's ChatML spec) and 'im' is generally read as input/instant message, not image marker.
deficits.fix: Correct or delete the etymology aside; state that ChatML was introduced with OpenAI's gpt-3.5-turbo ChatML documentation.

deficits.missing: The 'training-ready' ChatMLCollator with pack=True teaches block-diagonal masking and cu_seqlens earlier in the chapter but then emits only a token-level attention_mask computed as (batch_ids != pad_token_id), which (a) does not prevent the cross-document attention leakage the chapter warns about, and (b) zeroes out real EOS tokens whenever pad_token == eos_token, which is exactly the configuration chapter 5.1 recommends for Llama-style models.
deficits.fix: Have the collator also return per-row document_lengths (or cu_seqlens) and show one line wiring them into flash_attn_varlen_func or make_block_diagonal_mask; build attention_mask from sequence lengths rather than token-id comparison, or add a warning about the pad==eos collision.

deficits.missing: Cross-entropy in every training-context code path uses F.cross_entropy. The from-scratch numerically stable version exists only in foundations (1.4 stable log-softmax in torch; 1.6 fused softmax-CE with backward, in NumPy); the loss chapter never shows a from-scratch PyTorch cross_entropy over (B*T, V) logits.
deficits.fix: Add a short from-scratch `cross_entropy(logits, targets)` (gather of log_softmax via the logsumexp shift trick, ignore_index support) with an allclose check against F.cross_entropy, cross-referencing 1.4.

deficits.missing: Dead-expert/router-collapse is explained mechanistically (rich-get-richer loop, aux loss with runnable code, z-loss, expert-choice), but there is no monitoring snippet a reader can run during training to *detect* dying experts: no per-expert dispatch-fraction histogram, router-entropy metric, or alert threshold (e.g. expert receiving < 0.2/E of tokens for N steps).
deficits.fix: Add a ~20-line ExpertLoadMonitor that logs f_e per layer every K steps, router softmax entropy, and flags experts whose dispatch fraction stays below a threshold — mirroring the TrainingMonitor pattern already in 3.11.

deficits.missing: torch.autograd.set_detect_anomaly is never mentioned anywhere in the book; it is the standard first tool for localizing which op produced a NaN in the backward pass (complementing the forward-hook activation probe in 3.11).
deficits.fix: Add a short subsection or warning box in the debugging portion of 1.7 (cross-linked from 3.11's playbook STEP 3) showing `with torch.autograd.set_detect_anomaly(True):` usage and warning about its large overhead (debug-only).

deficits.missing: xformers is named in this unit's objectives but appears in the entire book only as one row of a backend-comparison table ('xFormers mem-efficient | Ampere+ | tiled attention'); a reader gets no sense of what memory_efficient_attention is, its historical role, or when to use it vs sdpa/flash-attn.
deficits.fix: Add a short paragraph (3-5 sentences) on xformers: memory_efficient_attention API, its Cutlass-based tiled kernel lineage, that PyTorch sdpa's EFFICIENT_ATTENTION backend descends from it, and that today it is mostly relevant for older GPUs and diffusion stacks.

deficits.missing: The duplicate BPE trainer in the appendix uses max(pairs, key=pairs.get) with a nondeterministic tie-break, directly contradicting the main chapter's (correct) insistence on deterministic tie-breaking; its worked example also asserts ('e','s') is 'most frequent' when several pairs tie at freq 2, and says 'Vocabulary shrinks by 2 entries' after a merge, which is not what happens (the per-word symbol sequences shorten; the word-frequency dict size is unchanged).
deficits.fix: Align the appendix snippet with the chapter version (deterministic tie-break), note the tie explicitly in the worked example, and fix the 'vocabulary shrinks' sentence.

deficits.missing: The einops library itself (rearrange/reduce/repeat named-axis notation) is never taught anywhere in the book, despite being a named objective of this unit (and a CS336 lecture-2 staple). It appears exactly once, unexplained, in 10-multimodal-and-arch/01-vision-transformers ('einops.rearrange' for patchify). torch.einsum IS taught well, so the functional gap is small.
deficits.fix: Add a short einops subsection next to the existing einsum section in 1.1 (or in 1.7): rearrange/reduce/repeat semantics, the named-axis pattern mini-language, equivalence to view/permute/reshape chains, and a multi-head-attention split/merge example (b s (h d) -> b h s d).

deficits.missing: The 'Watch the correction factor work' worked example deliberately presents a wrong reference value (1.00339), then reveals it was 'an arithmetic slip on the reader's behalf' and recomputes 1.010129. The self-correcting device reads as an uncaught erratum on first pass and risks confusing readers.
deficits.fix: Rewrite the example to compute the correct reference (1.010129) upfront, or clearly frame the wrong value as an intentional trap before presenting it.

deficits.missing: In the BlockManager sketch, append_slot()'s docstring says a new block is popped 'iff the last block just filled up', but the code pops a block from the free list unconditionally, and schedule_step() calls append_slot for every running sequence every decode step. As written, the sketch would allocate one fresh block per sequence per step (block_size-times over-allocation) instead of one per block_size steps. can_append() is also referenced but never defined.
deficits.fix: Pass the sequence's current token count into append_slot and only pop a block when count % block_size == 0 (mirroring the correct logic in 4.6's BlockManager.append_token); define can_append(seq) as free_blocks >= (1 if boundary else 0).

deficits.missing: The claim 'output tokens cost roughly 3-5x more compute per token than input tokens' is imprecise: per-token FLOPs are essentially identical (~2P) for prefill and decode tokens. The cost asymmetry comes from wall-clock/bandwidth (decode is memory-bound and poorly utilized per token), not from FLOPs.
deficits.fix: Reword to 'output tokens cost roughly 3-5x more in wall-clock GPU-time (and hence dollars) per token, because decode steps are bandwidth-bound and cannot amortize the weight read the way parallel prefill does; the FLOPs per token are the same.'

deficits.missing: The from-scratch speculative-sampling implementation re-runs draft and target from scratch each step (no KV cache, no rollback). The text explicitly flags this as an engineering note, but a cache-reusing variant with rollback of the rejected positions' KV is exactly the fiddly part a reader would need for a production-shaped implementation.
deficits.fix: Add a second listing (or exercise with solution sketch) that uses past_key_values for both models and shows how to truncate/roll back both caches to the accepted length after each speculative step, including the DynamicCache.crop / index-select mechanics.

deficits.missing: The chapter has excellent worked examples for REINFORCE-baseline variance and the PPO clip, but no hand-computed numeric check for compute_gae itself (the trickiest function: backward recursion, mask handling, terminal-reward placement).
deficits.fix: Add a 3-4 token toy example with explicit values/rewards and the expected delta_t, advantage, and return at each position, so a reader can unit-test their compute_gae against known numbers.

deficits.missing: The math benchmark section (MATH, GSM8K, AIME, FrontierMath, OlympiadBench) covers only informal, final-answer benchmarks; formal-proof benchmarks (miniF2F, ProofNet, PutnamBench) where verification is done by a proof checker rather than answer equivalence are not mentioned.
deficits.fix: Add one paragraph to the 'Benchmarks: MATH, GSM8K, AMC/AIME, MMLU-Pro' section contrasting informal answer-checked benchmarks with formally verified ones (miniF2F, PutnamBench), noting that the verifier is the Lean type-checker and that this eliminates the equivalence-checking false-negative problem discussed earlier in the same chapter.
